statement: A circuit must be a complete, unbroken path for the light to turn on.
explanation: True! Electricity can only flow through a complete circuit with no gaps.

statement: A metal key will stop the light from turning on in a circuit.
explanation: False! A metal key is a conductor. It completes the circuit and the light turns on.

statement: A rubber duck placed in a circuit will light up the bulb.
explanation: False! Rubber is an insulator. It breaks the circuit, so the light stays off.

statement: If there is a gap in a circuit, electricity can still flow through.
explanation: False! A gap breaks the circuit completely. Electricity needs an unbroken conductive path to flow.

statement: A cell stores electricity.
explanation: True! A cell stores electrical energy, typically at 1.5 volts.

statement: A battery is the same thing as a single cell.
explanation: False! A single unit is called a cell. Two or more cells connected together make a battery.

statement: A switch opens and closes a circuit.
explanation: True! A switch controls the flow of electricity by opening (breaking) or closing (completing) the circuit.

statement: A lamp holder connects the lamp to the circuit.
explanation: True! The lamp holder keeps the lamp in place and connects it to the wires so electricity can flow through.

statement: A circuit without a switch means the lamp can never turn on.
explanation: False! Without a switch, the circuit is always complete, so the lamp will stay on permanently with no easy way to turn it off.

statement: A conductor lets electricity move through it.
explanation: True! Conductors are materials that allow electricity to pass through freely. Metals are great conductors.

statement: An insulator lets electricity move through it easily.
explanation: False! An insulator blocks the flow of electricity. Materials like plastic, rubber, and wood are insulators.

statement: Plastic is a good conductor of electricity.
explanation: False! Plastic is an insulator. It blocks electricity, which is why it is used to cover wires and make appliance handles.

statement: The handles of electric appliances are insulators to keep you safe.
explanation: True! Appliance handles are made of insulating materials like plastic so you can safely hold them without getting an electric shock.

statement: The inside of a cable wire is an insulator.
explanation: False! The inside of a cable wire is metal, which is a conductor. The plastic covering on the outside is the insulator.

statement: Salt water is a conductor of electricity.
explanation: True! The dissolved salt in the water creates particles that help electricity flow through it.

statement: Tap water is an insulator.
explanation: False! Tap water is a conductor because it contains dissolved minerals that allow electricity to flow.

statement: Distilled water is a conductor just like tap water.
explanation: False! Distilled water is an insulator because it has been purified and has no minerals dissolved in it. Tap water conducts because of its minerals.

statement: The water in our body is an insulator.
explanation: False! The water in our body has dissolved minerals, making it a conductor. That is why we can get electric shocks.

statement: Switches turn appliances on and off.
explanation: True! Switches control the flow of electricity by completing or breaking the circuit.

statement: Pylons carry electricity from power stations to homes and schools.
explanation: True! Pylons are tall metal towers that support cables carrying electricity over long distances.

statement: Two cells connected together is called a battery.
explanation: True! A single unit is a cell. When you connect two or more cells, the combination is called a battery.

statement: Switches do not turn appliances on and off.
explanation: False! Switches are designed specifically to turn appliances on and off by opening and closing the circuit.

statement: A wall socket is where we plug in appliances to get electricity.
explanation: True! Wall sockets connect to the house wiring and provide mains electricity to appliances when plugged in.

statement: We measure the strength of electricity in kilograms.
explanation: False! The strength of electricity is measured in volts. Kilograms measure weight.

statement: Mains electricity is the low voltage electricity stored in batteries.
explanation: False! Mains electricity is high voltage power that comes from power stations. Battery electricity is low voltage.

statement: A cord is the plastic-covered wire that connects a plug to an appliance.
explanation: True! The cord carries electricity from the wall socket plug to the appliance safely inside its plastic cover.

statement: An electric shock only happens when you touch very large machines.
explanation: False! An electric shock can happen whenever a person touches any electrical energy source, even small household appliances and sockets.

statement: A cable is a single thin wire.
explanation: False! A cable is a rope of wires — multiple wires bundled together, often used to carry high-voltage electricity.

statement: Voltage measures how powerful electricity is.
explanation: True! Voltage is the measure of electrical power. Higher voltage means more powerful and potentially more dangerous electricity.

statement: You should always grip the plastic plug when pulling it out of a socket.
explanation: True! The plastic plug body is an insulator. Gripping it protects you from electricity.

statement: It is safe to pull a plug out by pulling the cord.
explanation: False! Pulling by the cord damages it and can expose bare wires, which can cause dangerous electric shocks.

statement: You should never touch the metal pins of a plug.
explanation: True! Metal pins are conductors. If connected to electricity, touching them could give you a dangerous shock.

statement: It is safe to use electrical appliances with wet hands.
explanation: False! Water is a conductor. Wet hands can create a path for electricity to flow through your body.

statement: Overloading a socket with too many plugs is dangerous.
explanation: True! Plugging too many appliances into one socket draws too much electricity and can cause overheating or fire.

statement: Bare wires are perfectly safe to touch.
explanation: False! Bare wires have lost their protective insulating cover. Touching them can cause a dangerous electric shock.

statement: You should stay away from pylons because they carry very high voltage electricity.
explanation: True! Pylons carry extremely powerful electricity. Getting too close to them is very dangerous.

statement: Running electrical cords under rugs is a safe practice.
explanation: False! Cords under rugs can overheat because the rug traps the heat. This can cause fires.

statement: Sticking a metal fork into a wall socket is extremely dangerous.
explanation: True! A metal fork is a conductor. Electricity from the socket would flow through the fork and into your body, causing a severe shock.

statement: Wood is a good conductor of electricity.
explanation: False! Wood is an insulator. It does not let electricity flow through it.

statement: Most metals are good conductors of electricity.
explanation: True! Metals like iron, copper, and gold all conduct electricity very well.

statement: A cork placed in a circuit will complete the circuit and light the bulb.
explanation: False! Cork is an insulator. It breaks the circuit, so the bulb will not light up.

statement: Electricity is generated at power stations and travels through cables on pylons.
explanation: True! Power stations generate electricity, which then travels along cables supported by pylons to reach homes and schools.

statement: A cable uses both a conductor (metal inside) and an insulator (plastic outside) for safety.
explanation: True! The metal inside carries electricity while the plastic outside prevents shocks. Both materials are essential for safe use.

statement: You can test if a material is a conductor by placing it in a circuit gap to see if the lamp turns on.
explanation: True! If the lamp lights up, the material completes the circuit and is a conductor. If it stays off, the material is an insulator.

statement: Wires carry electricity from one part of the circuit to another.
explanation: True! Wires are conductors that connect all the parts of the circuit, allowing electricity to flow through.

statement: A plug is a device for connecting to an electricity supply.
explanation: True! A plug connects an appliance's cord to a wall socket to access mains electricity.

statement: A single cell typically stores 1.5 volts of electricity.
explanation: True! Most standard cells (like AA or AAA) store 1.5 volts of electricity.

statement: It is safe to play near pylons.
explanation: False! Pylons carry extremely high voltage electricity. You should always stay far away from them.

statement: Rubber is a conductor of electricity.
explanation: False! Rubber is one of the best insulators. It blocks electricity completely, which is why it is used in protective gear.

statement: The human body is a conductor of electricity because it contains water with minerals.
explanation: True! The water in our bodies has dissolved minerals, making our bodies conduct electricity. This is why electric shocks are so dangerous to us.

statement: All electrical appliances produce heat energy when they work.
explanation: True! Every electrical appliance produces some heat when it operates, even if heat is not its main purpose.

statement: A food mixer transfers electrical energy into movement energy.
explanation: True! The motor in a food mixer converts electrical energy into movement (kinetic) energy to spin its blades.

statement: A speaker transfers electrical energy into light energy.
explanation: False! A speaker transfers electrical energy into sound energy, not light energy.

statement: When an object cools down, it transfers heat energy to its surroundings.
explanation: True! Heat energy moves from warmer objects to cooler surroundings until the temperatures even out.

statement: Energy can be created and destroyed.
explanation: False! The law of conservation of energy says that energy cannot be made, lost, used up, or destroyed — only transferred from one form to another.

statement: Adding an extra lamp to a circuit makes all the lamps glow brighter.
explanation: False! Adding more lamps means the energy is shared between more components, so each lamp glows dimmer.

statement: Adding an extra cell to a circuit makes the lamps glow brighter.
explanation: True! More cells provide more voltage (energy), which makes the lamps receive more power and glow brighter.

statement: Adding one cell and one lamp at the same time to a circuit keeps the brightness the same.
explanation: True! Adding both a cell and a lamp increases the power and the load equally, so the brightness stays balanced.

statement: If fewer people are vaccinated, the number of infections will decrease.
explanation: False! Fewer vaccinations mean less protection, so diseases spread more easily and infections increase.

statement: Safety caps on medicine bottles prevent young children from opening them.
explanation: True! Safety caps are specially designed to be difficult for young children to open, protecting them from accidentally taking medicine.

statement: You do not need goggles when doing experiments with chemicals.
explanation: False! You should always wear goggles during chemical experiments to protect your eyes from splashes.

statement: A lamp transfers electrical energy into light and heat energy.
explanation: True! A lamp's main purpose is to produce light, but it also produces heat energy as a side effect.
